figures.caption: Figure 6. Hydrogen permeability measured using SHIELD as a function of inverse temperature for 316 stainless steel, compared with published literature4,17–22. Literature values obtained from HTM23. Error bars represent propagated uncertainties from the pressurebased flux determination.
Figure 1. Schematic illustration of hydrogen permeation regimes under Pdown ≈ 0.

Figure 7. Hydrogen permeability measured using SHIELD as a function of inverse temperature for AISI 1018 low-carbon steel, compared with literature24. Literature values obtained from HTM23. Error bars represent propagated uncertainties from the pressure-based flux determination.

Figure 2. Schematic evolution of downstream pressure following application of an upstream pressure step. After an initial transient, a linear increase in downstream pressure is observed, corresponding to steady-state permeation. The slope of the linear regime is directly proportional to the permeation flux.
Figure 3. Schematic representation of the PI&D diagram of the SHIELD experimental platform

Figure 4. Permeation flux as a function of upstream hydrogen pressure for 316 stainless steel. At low pressures, the flux scales approximately linearly with pressure, indicating surface-limited transport. At higher pressures, the dependence approaches a square-root scaling, consistent with diffusion-limited permeation. The transition between regimes occurs at around 80 Torr in the present experimental configuration.
